coredata.dc:title: Progress in nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays of microcystins in seafood and aquaculture supply chains
coredata.dc:creator: He, Fengjiao
Nie, Chao
Liu, Sha
Wu, Guojian
Shen, Yizhong
Qiu, Nannan
Wu, Yongning
Liu, Xin
coredata.dc:description:
Background Microcystins (MCs) is toxic substances that may affect the safety and quality of seafood and aquatic products through water contamination, thus posing a potential threat to both human health and the ecosystem. When MCs exists in water, it can lead to contamination of aquatic plants and animals, which can affect other organisms in the food chain. Therefore, monitoring and controlling the level of MCs in water can ensure that seafood and aquatic products do not contain harmful substances, thereby safeguarding the health and safety of consumers. It also ensures that the quality of aquatic products meets hygiene standards and improves the market competitiveness of the products.
Scope and approach This article reviews the application of different fluorescence nanomaterials in the assays of MCs. In addition, the mechanism of fluorescence determination in MCs is introduced, including photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner-filter effect (IFE). Finally, we discussed the level of development and limitations of existing fluorescent nanotechnologies and suggested future possibilities in the detection of MCs.
Key finds and conclusions Fluorescence methods based on different nanomaterials can rapidly and accurately determine MCs in aqueous solution-related matrices, but the complexity of the environments relevant to the seafood and aquaculture chain limits the practical performance. The future development of multifunctional nanomaterials is needed to achieve a more stable and efficient portable method for the detection of MCs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: The limit of detection (LOD) is defined as the corresponding amount of three times the background signal of the instrument caused by matrix blank, or the average value of the background signal obtained from matrix blank plus three times the mean standard deviation. Usually, the smaller the LOD, the higher the sensitivity of the experiment.

Abbreviations: Au NPs: Gold nanoparticles; QDs: Quantum dots; Au NCs: Gold nanoclusters; CdTe-MSA: CdTe quantum dots capped with a mercaptosuccinic acid; CDs: Carbon dots; CDs plus N/P: N/P-Codoped Carbon Dots; CDs plus silane: Silane-doped Carbon dots; SWNTs plus dapoxy: Single-walled carbon nanotubes and dapoxyl; ZnTCPP@MOF: ZnTCPP-functionalized metal–organic framework; Cas14-pMOFs: CRISPR/Cas14a system integrated with 2D porphyrin metal-organic framework; ZnFe2O4-NP: Zinc ferrite nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Progress in nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays of microcystins in seafood and aquaculture supply chains

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microcystins (MCs) is toxic substances that may affect the safety and quality of seafood and aquatic products through water contamination, thus posing a potential threat to both human health and the ecosystem. When MCs exists in water, it can lead to contamination of aquatic plants and animals, which can affect other organisms in the food chain. Therefore, monitoring and controlling the level of MCs in water can ensure that seafood and aquatic products do not contain harmful substances, thereby safeguarding the health and safety of consumers. It also ensures that the quality of aquatic products meets hygiene standards and improves the market competitiveness of the products.

This article reviews the application of different fluorescence nanomaterials in the assays of MCs. In addition, the mechanism of fluorescence determination in MCs is introduced, including photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner-filter effect (IFE). Finally, we discussed the level of development and limitations of existing fluorescent nanotechnologies and suggested future possibilities in the detection of MCs.

Fluorescence methods based on different nanomaterials can rapidly and accurately determine MCs in aqueous solution-related matrices, but the complexity of the environments relevant to the seafood and aquaculture chain limits the practical performance. The future development of multifunctional nanomaterials is needed to achieve a more stable and efficient portable method for the detection of MCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the frequent occurrence of microcystins (MCs) blooms, the problem of water pollution in lakes and aquatic systems has seriously affected the quality and safety of seafood and aquaculture, thus directly threatening human health (Wei, et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2023). MCs is cyclic heptapeptides consisting of seven amino acids and is one of the most common cyanobacterial toxins found in freshwater environments, with different derived forms, such as microcystin-LR (MC-LR, it is a heptapeptide toxin containing alanine and glutamic acid, with special groups such as linolenic acid (Adda) and methacryloyl (Mdha) in its structure, Fig. 1 A), microcystin-RR (MC-RR, it is a heptapeptide toxin that also contains Adda and Mdha in its structure, but differs from MC-LR in the arrangement of amino acids, Fig. 1B), and microcystin-YR (MC-YR, its structure contains tyrosine and glutamic acid and differs in the arrangement of other amino acids, Fig. 1C) (Buratti, et al., 2017). MC-LR, MC-RR and MC-YR are three common derivatives of MCs that differ slightly in their chemical structures and therefore may have different effects on organisms. MC-LR is highly toxic, it may cause acute toxicity symptoms and prolonged exposure may result in liver damage and neurological damage; MC-YR is structurally somewhat similar to MC-LR but may be slightly less toxic; MC-RR is structurally different from MC-LR and MC-YR and may have different toxicological manifestations, it may also cause acute toxicity symptoms but the effects on the liver and the nervous system may be different (Zhang et al., 2019). The chemical structural features of MCs include a cyclic polypeptide structure and a phosphite structure containing an α-carbonyl group. These chemical features make MCs highly toxic and biologically active. MCs can cause serious health effects in humans and animals, for example, ingestion of MCs can lead to acute symptoms of toxicity, and prolonged exposure can lead to chronic toxicity, in severe cases, liver damage, nerve damage and even carcinogenicity can occur. MCs also has a negative impact on aquatic organisms and ecosystems, disrupting water quality and ecological balance (Marques, Maulvault, & Nunes, 2018; Welten, Meneely, & Elliott, 2020). Recent studies have shown that toxins released by MCs blooms accumulate in water bodies and enter the food chain through bioconcentration, affecting the health of crops and aquatic animals, and ultimately posing a potential risk to human dietary safety, as well as the magnitude of the problem demonstrated that water pollution was closely related to food safety (Mahmud, Valizadeh, Oyom, & Tahergorabi, 2024; Xu et al., 2021; Zhang, Zhu, et al., 2019). To decline the risk of MCs, the World Health Organization (WHO) recommended that the concentration of MCs in drinking water should not be greater than 1.0 μg/L (Chen, Giesy, & Xie, 2017). Early assays of MCs can help to combat this highly toxic group of substances and prevent them from causing greater harm to the ecosystem and human health (Massey, et al., 2020). Conventional methods for the assays of MCs are mainly gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), protein phosphatase inhibition (PPI), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Liu, Li, Zhang, Liu, & Xie, 2022). GC-MS can quickly and accurately detect MCs through tedious steps, but the sample pretreatment is complex, and the assay results are greatly affected by environmental factors. LC-MS assay of MCs has high sensitivity and selectivity, and can detect multiple samples at the same time. However, the instruments used are relatively expensive and require high operational requirements from the assay personnel, resulting in high assay costs (Kordasht, et al., 2020). PPI can produce a highly sensitive response to MCs and help detect low concentrations of the toxin. However, PPI may require complex experimental manipulation and data analysis, adding to the complexity of the experiment. Some PPI may require long experimental times and are not suitable for situations where rapid detection is required (Sassolas, Catanante, Fournier, & Marty, 2011). ELISA is a fast and simple method that can perform quantitative and qualitative analysis. It has been widely used in the assays of MCs, but it requires complex measurement steps and time-consuming assay processes (Park, et al., 2021). This is not only time-consuming for simple on-site detection methods, but also incompatible with rapid, efficient and high-throughput screening methods (Hou, et al., 2016). In addition to these methods mentioned above, the use of non-fluorescent materials or simple organic fluorescent molecules in the detection of MCs, or the use of sensors with different registration parameters is also an innovative approach with certain advantages and features (Lee et al., 2016). Therefore, effective MCs assays should be developed to protect seafood and aquaculture safety.

Compared with traditional methods (LC-MS, GC-MS), new nanotechnology-based fluorescence analysis methods perform better in terms of detection limit and linearity. Using Surface Enhanced Fluorescence (SEF) technology, the strong electromagnetic field generated on the surface of nanostructures can significantly enhance the fluorescence signals, thus improving the detection sensitivity. This technique not only enables the detection of low concentrations of target substances, but also provides a wider linear range, resulting in more accurate and reliable results at different concentrations. Fluorescence assays have advantages such as sensitivity, accuracy, and visualization, and can be combined with other mature methods, greatly reducing the assay process and cycle of MCs (Liu, Niu, Wang, Ma, & Lin, 2018). At present, a number of fluorescent sensor products for MCs monitoring are commercially available. These fluorescent sensors are typically based on nanomaterials and fluorescent probe technology, and are capable of rapidly and accurately detecting MCs. Commercially available fluorescent sensor products can be used to monitor MCs concentrations in real time, contributing to public health. In the fluorescence assays of MCs, the optical, electronic, and chemical properties of fluorescence nanomaterials can improve the sensitivity and resolution of fluorescence assays, thereby achieving high selectivity assays of MCs (Hassan, et al., 2020; Kordasht et al., 2020; Vogiazi et al., 2019). Therefore, the application of fluorescence nanomaterials in the assays of MCs is of great significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms in nanotechnology-driven innovative fluorescence assays toward MCs
ce:section.ce:para: In the process of fluorescence assays of MCs, different mechanisms of action correspond to different methods, and have a significant impact on the linear range and detection limit of results. Therefore, in order to better understand the mechanism of fluorescence determination of MCs based on nanomaterials, we summarize the current progress about the various fluorescence mechanisms and their occurrence characteristics for the determination of MCs in seafood and aquaculture. Among the many fluorescence assays, according to the different mechanisms of action, fluorescence assays could be divided into PET, FRET and IFE.

PET refers to the fluorescence quenching of another compound by electron transfer when one compound is excited. This mechanism is of great significance in the design of fluorescence assays, as it can regulate the fluorescence signal by changing the concentration of specific compounds in the sample, thereby achieving rapid and sensitive assays of target molecules. PET has attracted much attention due to its ability to prepare high-energy and highly active compounds (Dogutan & Nocera, 2019; Lewis-Borrell, Sneha, Bhattacherjee, Clark, & Orr-Ewing, 2020; Stojanović et al., 2021). The entire PET cycle consists of two charge transfers. In a closed shell system, there are mainly two processes involved: (1) photo induced charge separation (CS); (2) subsequent thermal charge recombination (CR) (Fig. 3 A). When obtaining higher free ion yields, fine regulation must be carried out in both fast CS and slow CR reactions to obtain larger free ion yields (Vauthey, 2022). Considering their important role in many aspects, people have begun to delve into this reaction.

It has been shown that nanoparticles of different compositions initiate a system with co-initiators, solvents, or monomers that are very similar to the mechanism of free radical polymerization (Zhang, Yang, Bao, Wu, & Wang, 2013). In addition, the optical properties of nanoparticles were achieved by altering their morphology, size, and surface modification (Alivisatos, 1996). In this case, Gong et al. constructed a silver ion modified gold nanocluster ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe based on PET. The ratiometric fluorescence probe could exhibit a naked-eye visible color change in response to the target visible from red to purple and then to blue, which facilitated visual determination and provided good recognition of real wastewater samples (Gong, et al., 2023).

From the above examples, it is easy to see that PET is an important fluorescence quenching mechanism that has been widely applied in many fields. In recent years, researchers have developed many methods for MCs using the PET mechanism. These fluorescence methods can be designed for specific target molecules, achieving fluorescence signal quenching or enhancement through PET, thereby achieving quantitative assay of target molecules. In addition, the PET mechanism can also be used to construct ratiometric fluorescence probes, which can achieve sensitive assay of target MCs molecules by comparing the fluorescence signal changes of different wavelengths. In summary, the PET mechanism is of great significance in the fluorescence assays of MCs in seafood and aquaculture-related matrices.

FRET is a non-radioactive energy transfer by long-distance dipole-dipole interactions between donor and acceptor pairs (Wu, et al., 2020). In FRET, an excited molecule (acceptor) transfers its energy to another neighboring molecule (donor) through non radiative energy transfer, causing the donor's excited state to transition to the acceptor's excited state, while the acceptor emits fluorescence (Shi, Tian, Lyu, & Yang, 2015). The premise for this process to occur is that the distance between the donor and acceptor is within the range of 1.0–10.0 nm (Fig. 3B). Therefore, FRET is commonly used to study molecular interactions, protein folding states, and intracellular molecular localization (Yang, Li, Ni, & Liu, 2023). At present, food safety research based on the FRET process has become the most promising research direction (Shen, Nie, et al., 2022). Especially, energy transfer between donor and acceptor is achieved using FRET technology, which can easily generate larger luminescence wavelengths, reduce self-absorption and background interference, and achieve high signal-to-noise ratios and high sensitivities (Wu, et al., 2020). More importantly, FRET-based assays have the potential to detect MCs, providing new ideas for the rapid assays of MCs in water bodies and aquatic products (Kaur & Dhakal, 2019; Shen et al., 2021).

Generally, FRET-based nanomaterials can be achieved by self-assembly of different types of nanomaterials, or by modification of small organic molecules. In this case, a typical example was, Shi et al. developed a method for detecting MCs based on the fluorescence quenching of graphene oxide (GO) induced by gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) through FRET (Shi, et al., 2012). The detection limits for MC-LR and MC-RR were 5.0 × 102 μg/L and 3.0 × 102 μg/L. This method exhibited great selectivity towards MCs, while showing almost no response to other toxins. In addition, theoretically, all fluorescence groups can serve as FRET donors. After the donor molecule is recognized, its absorption spectrum needs to coincide with the donor's emission spectrum. On this basis, the receptor can be a fluorescent substance or a quenching agent (Shen, Wei, Zhu, Cao, & Han, 2022). A representative example was, Wu et al. constructed a FRET-based detection method for algal toxins using upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) as donors and fluorescent bursting agents (BHQ1, BHQ3) as the corresponding acceptors (Wu, Duan, Zhang, & Wang, 2014). Among them, BHQ1 and BHQ3 are a class of commonly used fluorescence quenching agents, which belong to the quinoline class of compounds and have specific structures that can effectively quench fluorescent signals. And they can absorb light energy in a specific wavelength range and then release the energy by non-radiative leaping, thus quenching the fluorescence signal. The research has found that quenching agent probes could effectively quench green and red up-conversion fluorescence, due to the strong overlap relationship between fluorescence quenching agents and fluorescent dyes. The fluorescence intensity of MC-LR was linearly related to the concentration of the algal toxin between 0.1 and 50.0 μg/L.

In FRET, the closer the donor and acceptor are to each other, the stronger the Coulomb force between them and the more efficient the energy transfer. Therefore, FRET can be used to measure the distance between molecules and detect changes in the interactions between molecules. In addition, researchers have implemented fluorescence methods for MCs by using self-assembly of different types of nanomaterials or modification of organic small molecules. These studies provide new ideas for MCs assays based on FRET technology and have broad application prospects.

The principle of IFE is that certain components in the sample (such as proteins, dyes, etc.) can absorb or scatter excitation light, thereby weakening or eliminating the excitation light of fluorescent molecules (Fig. 3C). This absorption or scattering effect is related to the concentration, absorption spectrum, and fluorescence spectrum in the sample (Li, Fu, Liu, Li, & Huang, 2022). However, at high concentrations, there is a non-linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and fluorophore concentration, which is mainly due to the non-uniformity of the IFE fluorescence intensity because the excitation wavelength is significantly absorbed by fluorophores or other chromophores (Wang, Zeng, & Li, 2017).

The IFE is an intermolecular process in which at least two different molecules undergo energy transfer (Al-Hashimi, Omer, & Rahman, 2020; Ma et al., 2017; Suzuki & Yokoyama, 2015). In this case, a typical example was, Le et al. constructed a simple and highly selective fluorescence assays system using nitrogen phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) combined with gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) (Le, Kim, & Park, 2021). The fluorescence emission spectra of Au NPs and NPCDs overlap, resulting in effective internal filter and fluorescence quenching. In addition, another typical example was, Xiong et al. introduced arginine modified fluorescence enhanced gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) into the reaction system, utilizing the perfect overlap between the light absorption of silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and the excitation energy spectrum of Au NCs to effectively convert color signals into fluorescence signals through IFE (Xiong, et al., 2022). The fluorescence quenching efficiency decreases with the increase of the concentration of the tested substance.

The assays of MCs using the IFE usually involves binding the MCs in the sample with specific fluorescence probes or dyes to generate fluorescent signals. However, there may be other components in the sample (such as proteins, dissolved organic compounds, etc.), leading to signal quenching or distortion. In order to overcome the impact of internal filtration on MCs assays, researchers usually use internal filtration correction methods (Li, et al., 2022). Through these calibration methods, the fluorescence signal of MCs in the sample can be accurately measured, thereby achieving accurate assays of its concentration. The development of these methods provides important technical support for seafood supply chains monitoring and aquaculture supply chains safety, helping to timely and accurately assay of MCs, ensuring water quality, seafood and aquaculture safety, and public health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials-based innovative fluorescence assays toward MCs
ce:section.ce:para: As a highly sensitive, selective and real-time monitoring technique, fluorescence detection can be an effective tool to quickly and accurately detect the presence of MCs for timely response. Although fluorescence detection based on nanomaterials has some disadvantages, such as complex preparation and modification and high production cost, it still has the following advantages over conventional fluorescence detection: (1) Nanomaterials can increase the fluorescence signal intensity and enhance the detection sensitivity. (2) The specific structure of nanomaterials can achieve highly selective detection of the target molecules. (3) Nanomaterials can be combined with different functional units to achieve multifunctional detection. (4) Nanomaterials have rapid response characteristics, which can achieve real-time monitoring and rapid detection. (5) Some of the nanomaterials have good biocompatibility and are suitable for biological sample detection applications. At present, fluorescence nanomaterials have shown very broad prospects in the detection of MCs due to their good photostability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. So far, many fluorescence nanomaterials have been reported for the determination of MCs (such as gold nanomaterials, QDs, carbon nanomaterials, metal-organic skeletons (MOFs), and other nanomaterials). In the following section, we will systematically discuss fluorescence nanomaterials for MCs assays, which will better promote progress in the field of MCs assays and ensure the safety of environmental media and human health.

Au NPs are one of the older nanomaterials studied, often referred to as colloidal gold in biological studies, and their particle sizes typically range from 1.0 to 100.0 nm. Numerous studies have shown that colloidal gold can adsorb proteins stably and rapidly, and the biological activity of proteins does not change significantly (Yan et al., 2018). It can be used as a probe to locate biomolecules such as polysaccharides, proteins, peptides, antigens, hormones and nucleic acids. Au NPs, as a class of precious metal nanoparticles in nanomaterials, have their own unique properties in addition to the universal properties of nanomaterials. For analytical purposes, AuNPs can be used in areas such as SERS detection, electrochemical sensing, biomarker detection, etc., and their unique properties make them an important analytical tool. Compared with the application of AuNPs alone, the integration of fluorescence technology with AuNPs can bring additional advantages, including: (1) Fluorescently labelled AuNPs can be detected not only by fluorescence signals, but also by using the nature of the AuNPs themselves to perform SERS or electrochemical analyses, which can achieve dual-signal detection and improve the accuracy and reliability of detection. (2) Modification of different fluorescent dyes on the surface of gold nanoparticles can achieve multiple labelling and expand its scope of application. (3) The combination of fluorescence technology and AuNPs can use the surface enhancement effect of AuNPs to enhance the fluorescence signal and improve the detection sensitivity and resolution. In conclusion, the integration of fluorescence technology with AuNPs can not only make full use of the basic characteristics of AuNPs for analysis, but also bring additional advantages to improve the efficiency and accuracy of analysis, which have a broad application prospect in MCs assays.

Surface-Enhanced Fluorescence (SEF) refers to the phenomenon where a robust electromagnetic field produced on a nanoparticle's surface leads to notable alterations in the state of fluorescent molecules in the vicinity of nanorods or nano-intersections, resulting in enhanced intensity and stability of the fluorescent signal (Dragan, et al., 2012). Based on this, Sun's group constructed a fluorescence method based on SEF using Au NCs as fluorescence enhanced nanoparticles and cy5 as fluorescence markers (Li, et al., 2014). MC-LR-BSA and MC-LR rabbit antibody act as a spacer layer between the Au NCs and the cy5 molecule. The MC-LR-BSA can compete with the MC-LR antigen to bind the MC-LR rabbit antibody. The amount of cy5-labelled goat anti-rabbit IgG that binds to the MC-LR rabbit antibody depends on the concentration of the free MC-LR antigen. Therefore, as the concentration of free MC-LR antigen increases, the amount of cy5-labelled goat anti-rabbit antibody assembled decreases. MC-LR can be detected using fluorescence enhancement of Au NCs. The linear range of the method was 0.02–16.0 μg/L, and the detection limit was 0.007 μg/L (Fig. 4 A). The method achieved high recoveries by extracting contaminated fish samples through methanol. SEF technology is expected to be used for real-time assays of MC-LR in seafood. However, fluorescence measurements indicate that SEF is influenced by the size, shape, and distribution of Au NPs. The characteristic peak positions and morphology of Au NPs vary with particle spacing, particle size, surrounding environment, etc. Based on this, the surface modification of Au NPs is carried out to make them have specific functions. The method is simple, fast, inexpensive, and can even be observed directly with the naked eye. Inspired by the above, Cao et al. established a fluorescence methods for MCs based on the IFE between Au NPs and MoS2 QDs (Cao, et al., 2020). By utilizing the high affinity of nucleic acid aptamers for MC-LR, Au NPs modified by nucleic acid aptamers were aggregated in salt solution and blocked by IFE, achieving their up-conversion fluorescence with long-wave excitation (∼862 nm) and short-wave emission (∼505 nm), avoiding unwanted background signal interference (Fig. 4B). Where a nucleic acid aptamer is a molecule consisting of a DNA or RNA molecule that has the ability to bind specifically to a target molecule. The fluorescence method developed in this study can specifically detects MC-LR in filtered ambient water samples, which provided an alternative method for constructing portable devices for real-time determination of MC-LR. The linear range of the method was 0.05–40.99 μg/L, and the detection limit was 1.02 × 10−2 μg/L. This proposed method achieves high sensitivity and rapid detection of MC in real ambient water samples, but due to the complexity of ambient water, there may be other corrosive substances or other background signals interfering with the accuracy of the method.

To address this issue, the use of fluorescence microscopy imaging technology by many researchers to visualize nanoscale biochemical mechanisms has attracted widespread attention. Xie et al. provided a simple and effective method to visual the enzyme shear kinetics process (Xie, et al., 2021). Based on nanopipettes, a simple and effective fluorescence platform was established, and Au NPs were designed to protect aptamer (50-GGC GCC AAA CAG GAC CAC CAT GAC AAT TAC CCA TAC CAC CTC ATT ATG CCC CAT CTC CGC-30) and used for the sensitive assays of MC-LR (Fig. 4C). The aptamer binds specifically to MC-LR and leaves the surface of Au NPs. With the addition of deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I) enzyme, the aptamer is selectively cleaved and MC-LR is released as an additional target molecule to achieve fluorescence intensity signal amplification. The platform, which allowed continuous loading of swollen biological and chemical molecules in a nanoscale environment, had been successfully used to manipulate the response process of the protective effect of Au NPs on DNA cytoplasmic digestion. The linear range of the method was 25.5 to 20.4 μg/L. Real-time imaging of each step was achieved by modulating the interaction of the nucleic acid aptamer/Au NPs with MC-LR and DNase I. This method will provide a new approach for research in the fields of biology, chemistry and materials.

The surface modification of Au NPs using nucleic acid aptamers, monoclonal antibodies, and other biomolecules has been shown to enhance their specificity and affinity for MCs, leading to various fluorescence detection methods. These methods have the characteristics of simple operation, speed, low cost, and strong portability, and are expected to achieve real-time on-site detection of MCs in the environment and food. However, there are still some challenges in the analysis of gold nanomaterials in MCs. For example, the synthesis and functionalization of gold nanomaterials often require complex experimental conditions and techniques, which increase costs and operational difficulties. In addition, gold nanomaterials may be interfered by other components in the actual sample, such as dissolved organic matter, proteins, etc., which may cause signal quenching or distortion. The stability and storage conditions of gold nanomaterials are also issues, and appropriate storage conditions need to be found to maintain their performance. Despite these challenges, gold nanomaterials still have enormous development potential in the field of MCs detection. Research has shown that further exploration of the composite application of gold nanomaterials with other nanomaterials can improve the specificity and sensitivity of detection. By combining nanotechnology and biotechnology, it is expected to develop more intelligent and portable MCs detection devices to meet the needs of practical applications.

From the above description, it can be seen that gold nanomaterials exhibit excellent performance in the assays of MCs. However, compared to gold nanomaterials, QDs have superior optical performance, size controllability and versatility (Liu, et al., 2019; Zhang, Zhu, et al., 2019; Zhang, Liu, Liu, & Li, 2022). QDs are a type of semiconductor nanostructure with dimensions at the nanoscale, typically ranging from 1.0 to 10.0 nm (Ghaffarkhah, et al., 2021). QDs produce optical signals based on quantum mechanical effects and the nature of semiconductor materials. When the size of semiconductor materials is reduced to the nanoscale, electrons and holes are confined, leading to a unique set of optical and electrical properties, which make QDs have broad application prospects in the field of detecting MCs (Ahmad, Aslam, Mustafa, Jamil, & Ahmad, 2018). At present, researchers have investigated QDs with different structures and compositions and reported some QDs in the determination of MCs. QDs with different chemical compositions, such as CdSe, ZnSe, and CdTe, have unique properties as analytical labels: (1) CdSe have good photostability and luminescence efficiency, which are suitable for long-time real-time imaging and detection. (2) ZnSe have excellent performance in the ultraviolet region and can be used for fluorescent labelling and detection at specific wavelengths. (3) CdTe emit light in the visible light range and are suitable for visible light imaging and labelling (Bae, Myung, & Bard, 2004). Therefore, by detecting the fluorescence signal changes of QDs, high sensitivity and selectivity assays of MCs can be achieved, and this method is expected to become an important technical means in the field of MCs monitoring.

In recent years, it has been found that the use of fluorescence labelling instead of colorimetric labelling can significantly reduce its detection limit (1.0–2.0 orders of magnitude) (Taranova, Berlina, Zherdev, & Dzantiev, 2014). QDs have become an ideal fluorescence marker for a wide range of applications due to their advantages of unity excitation, adjustable fluorescence wavelength, high fluorescence intensity, and strong resistance to photobleaching. Based on this, Sun et al. used immunochromatographic test strips (ICTS) to establish a MC-LR method (Sun, et al., 2017). In the ICTS system, QDs were used as fluorescence markers and MC-LR was used as the target, the linear range of the method was 0.25–5.0 μg/L and a detection limit was 0.1 μg/L under the experimental conditions (Fig. 5 A). The method can be used for the spiked detection of MC-LR in tap water, river water and lake water. Another strategy is to use fluorescence spectra and changes in the fluorescence of microalgae itself as screening tools. Kalnaitytė et al. studied the effect of CdTe-MSA on MCs. QDs were used as fluorescence markers in biological systems, and their fluorescence spectra (PL) and changes in own fluorescence (AF) could be used as a non-destructive means of screening the interaction between nanomaterials and algal cells in the natural environment (Kalnaitytė & Bagdonas, 2019). Mercaptosuccinic acid-coated CdTe were synthesized and modified with a layer of aqueous-phase ionic media to enhance their stability and biocompatibility. The CdTe-MSA were contacted with MCs solution to monitor the changes in the position of the fluorescence emission peak (∼550 nm) as well as the fluorescence properties with the ionic aqueous solution. By observing the changes in the PL and AF of the CdTe-MSA, the interaction between MCs and the QDs can be understood, and thus the presence and concentration of MCs can be detected. Based on the changes in the fluorescence properties of CdTe-MSA, MCs can be detected quickly and accurately and their levels in the environment can be assessed.

As shown in the above example, QDs can be used as fluorescence markers for detecting MCs, providing high sensitivity and long-term stable fluorescence signals. However, the specificity of individual QDs for detecting MCs is not strong. In order to improve the practical application of QDs, many researchers have proposed combining QDs with ligands to achieve highly selective recognition and assays of specific biomolecules, with high specificity. FRET is a radiation-free energy transfer process between an excited group and an acceptor. The energy transfer can only take place when the donor-acceptor spacing reaches 10.0–100.0 Å. Therefore, FRET is a precise and highly sensitive method for the assays of intermolecular interactions. Representatively, Eun-Hee Lee and Ahjeong Son proposed to combine nucleic acid aptamers with FRET technology to achieve highly sensitive and specific assays of MC-LR toxins (Lee and Son, 2019). The single stranded (ssDNA) aptamer AN6 (5′-NH2-GGC GCC AAA CAG GAC CAC CAT GAC AAT TAC CCA TAC CAC CTC ATT ATG CCC CAT CTC CGC-3′) was used for the detection of MC-LR. The interaction between nucleic acid aptamers and MC-LR toxins was used to change the donor (MB-QD525) and acceptor (PoPo3 dye) distance, which in turn leaded to changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5B). In this method, a rapid determination of MC-LR under laboratory conditions was achieved, but other factors that may affect the results include chemical solution composition, temperature, and ionic strength.

It is not difficult to see that the use of QDs has become a promising strategy for developing fluorescence method for detecting MCs. However, QDs also have some drawbacks, such as potential toxicity and biocompatibility issues, especially when applied in living organisms, which may have adverse effects on cells and tissues. Therefore, more toxicology and biocompatibility studies are needed. In addition, the current high preparation cost of QDs may limit their practical application and require further cost reduction. In summary, although QDs have some drawbacks in the application of MCs assays, with the continuous progress and improvement of technology, QDs still have broad application prospects, especially in high sensitivity and selectivity of MCs assays, which is expected to become an important technical means.

Elemental carbon is one of the most closely related and important elements in nature for human beings, and therefore its uses are well-known and extensively reported (Bhaisare, Gedda, Khan, & Wu, 2016; Shi et al., 2018). Compared with QDs and other fluorescence nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials have many other advantages, such as low biological toxicity, stable chemical properties, and a wide range of sources (Luo, et al., 2019; Shen, Gao, et al., 2023). Therefore, carbon materials with carbon as the only constituent element have a variety of properties, and new carbon phases and carbon materials continue to be discovered and artificially produced (He, Sun, Wu, Pu, & Wei, 2021; Shen, Nie, et al., 2023). Carbon nanomaterials have excellent electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, many functional groups, high conductivity and good electrocatalytic activity, which can be widely used in the fields of MCs assays (Mishra, et al., 2022; Singh, Pandey, Singh, & Singh, 2021; Tokula et al., 2023).

Single carbon nanotubes have the advantages of light weight, good thermal stability, and large specific surface area, which are widely used in the preparation of a variety of methods. In addition, this graphitic substance has a good quenching effect on fluorophores. For example, Seyed Mohammad Taghdisi et al. developed a fluorescence assays based on single-walled carbon nanotubes and dapoxy for the assays of MC-LR (Taghdisi, et al., 2017). Single-walled carbon nanotubes were used as the carrier, dapoxy as the fluorescent dye, DAP-10 as the specific nucleic acid aptamer, and non-modified MC-LR nucleic acid aptamer (Apt) as the ligand targeting to recognize dapoxy (Fig. 6 A). The MC-LR aptamer (Apt) (5′-GGC GCC AAA CAG GAC CAC CAT GAC AAT TAC CCA TAC CAC CTC ATT ATG CCC CAT CTC CGC-3′), FAM-labelled Apt (5′-FAM-GGC GCC AAA CAG GAC CAC CAT GAC AAT TAC CCA TAC CAC CTC ATT ATG CCC CAT CTC CGC-3′) and dapoxyl aptamer (DAP-10) (5′-CAA TTA CGG GGG AGG GTG TGT GGT CTT GCT TGG TTC GTA TTG-3′) were used for the detection of MC-LR. When MC-LR was absent, dapoxy could interact with DAP-10, resulting in strong fluorescence intensity. When MC-LR was present, DAP-10 adsorbed on the surface of single-walled carbon nanotubes, giving it a low fluorescence intensity. Optimization based on experimental conditions, a highly specific assays of MC-LR with a detection limit of 0.14 μg/L was achieved. Meanwhile, the proposed method could achieve the accurate assays of MC-LR in the real environment.

However, carbon nanotubes still have some limitations in detecting MCs compared to carbon dots (CDs). They have high preparation costs, poor dispersibility, single structure and morphology, and fewer functional groups. In comparison, CDs may have more advantages. CDs have become a research hotspot in the field of bio-assays and imaging due to their low cost, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and high luminescence efficiency. Representatively, Xu et al. established a fluorescence enzyme-linked immunoassay (FELISA) based on silane-doped CDs for the highly sensitive assays of MC-LR in water and crucian carp (Xu, et al., 2020). A horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-labelled MC-LR polyclonal antibody was used as a signal probe to bind to the substance to be detected or the encapsulated antigen (Fig. 6B). The HRP-catalyzed 2,20-azinobis-ethyl benzothiazoline-6-sulfonate (ABTS) oxidation product (ox-ABTS) effectively induced the fluorescence burst of subsequently added Si-CDs, and the quantitative detection of MC-LR was achieved based on the change of fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs, which showed good linearity between 1.0 × 103 to 3.20l × 106 μg/L, and its lower detection limit was 6.0 × 10−4 μg/L. The new FELISA technique proposed in this study could improve the assays sensitivity and avoid the complicated biochemical coupling process, which was expected to expand its application in food safety monitoring. In addition, for the unique chemical structure of MCs, indirect sensing strategies can be designed for MCs. As a representative example, Wang et al. developed a fluorescence method for the assays of MC-LR based on the properties of N/P-doped CDs (Wang, et al., 2021). In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence of N/P CDs was quenched due to the formation of Fe–O–P bonds between Fe3+ and the phosphate groups of the CDs. This system had high sensitivity to MC-LR and was able to restore fluorescence rapidly by competing for the fit with Fe3+. The method had a wide linear range of 0.05–3.0 μg/L for MC-LR and a detection limit of 1.71 × 10−2 μg/L. This was a new analytical strategy that could lay the foundation for the rapid assays of MCs in environmental samples. However, although the above methods can improve the photostability of CDs, there may still be issues with light attenuation, which can affect their stability in long-term imaging and sensing applications. In order to facilitate rapid assay in practical applications, it is necessary to select fluorescence markers with excellent doping and combine them with CDs to provide strong fluorescence signals. For example, molecular imprinting technology (MIT) generates artificial binding sites that are precisely complementary to the substrate, enabling fine recognition of target molecules (Ostovan, et al., 2022). MIT uses template molecules to guide the polymerization of functional monomers and cross-linkers to form polymers, and removes the template to leave pores that match the structure of the template molecules to achieve specific recognition of the target molecule (Arabi, et al., 2021; Arabi et al., 2022). In this case, a typical example was that Li et al. constructed a new method for highly sensitive proportional fluorescence assays of MCs based on virtual molecularly imprinted polymers using dimethyl-biguanide as a template (Li, Fu, et al., 2023). CDs were used as auxiliary sensing signals, and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was used as the reference enhancement signal. The fluorescence assays of MCs could be achieved by using the significant fluorescence quenching effect of MCs on CDs and the reference fluorescence enhancement of MCs by FITC-SiO2. This method had good sensitivity, stability and selectivity, and the detection limits were 0.013 μg/L for MC-RR and 0.022 μg/L for MC-LR. The results indicated that the linear range of MCs was 0.5–500.0 μg/L.

Carbon nanomaterials have made significant progress in the determination of MCs due to their unique characteristics, including low cost, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and high luminescence efficiency. However, some carbon nanomaterials still have the following drawbacks when applied to MCs detection: (1) They may have lower detection sensitivity, which may limit their application in MCs assay. (2) Nonspecific signals may be generated in complex biological samples, which can affect the accuracy of assay results. There may be biocompatibility issues, especially when applied in living organisms, which may have adverse effects on cells and tissues. For these existing problems, we can improve the assay sensitivity of carbon nanomaterials through surface modification, structural design, and other means, further enhancing the surface functionalization of carbon nanomaterials to improve their specificity in complex samples and reduce non-specific signal interference, enabling their more effective application in MCs assays. Overall, the innovative application of carbon nanomaterials in MCs assay has brought broad prospects for environmental monitoring and food safety, providing fast and reliable assays, and has been widely applied in various fields.

MOFs are crystalline porous materials formed by interconnecting inorganic metal (metal ions or metal clusters) with bridging organic ligands through self-assembly (Jiao, Seow, Skinner, Wang, & Jiang, 2019). In addition, MOFs are ideal sensing materials for the analysis of target samples through surface reactions, and can be used for efficient adsorption or separation of analytes with high porosity, uniform porosity, and large specific surface area (Koo, Jang, & Kim, 2019; Kumar et al., 2017; Sharanyakanth & Radhakrishnan, 2020). The assembly of MOFs with other functional materials (e.g., ionic liquids, QDs, graphene, magnetic nanoparticles, Au, Ag, etc.) can endow MOFs with excellent conductive, luminescent, magnetic, catalytic, and other properties. The use of MOFs and their composites as sample pre-treatment or sensing materials for chromatography in the assays of microbial toxins is a promising research direction (Kathuria, Badawy, Al-Ghamdi, Hamachi, & Kivy, 2023; Zhang et al., 2021).

MOFs have excellent fluorescence quenching properties and good biocompatibility, and are expected to be an ideal fluorescence quencher in the CRISPR/Cas system. In this case, a typical example was that Wu et al. developed a novel universal fluorescence assays for MC-LR in tap and drinking water (Wu, et al., 2022). The main component was the integration of the CRISPR/Cas14a system with 2D porphyrin metal-organic framework nanosheets (2D-pMOFs), which could be activated by unconjugated DNA (cDNA) and positively correlated with the MC-LR expression level (Fig. 7 A). Subsequently, activated Cas14a indiscriminately cleaved FAM-labelled ssDNA (ssDNA-FAM) pre-adsorbed on the surface of 2D-pMOFs. The cleaved DNA fragments were readily desorbed from the 2D-pMOFs and recovered the fluorescence signal. The method had a wide linear range of 5.0 × 10−2 − 1.0 × 103 μg/L and a detection limit was 1.9 × 10−2 μg/L. The Cas14-pMOFs constructed in this study is not only suitable for DNA targets, but also could be applied to the assays of other important targets. However, such MOFs suffered from low fluorescence quenching efficiency and long quenching time. To overcome this problem, many researchers use adapters to detect MCs. Adapters can achieve efficient fluorescence quenching efficiency through specific binding, and this fast quench time can be used for real-time monitoring of MCs. In this regard, Su et al. developed a one-step electrodeposition-assisted self-assembly technique to prepare ZnTCPP@MOF and used it to prepare a fluorescence method for MC-LR (Su et al., 2023). The aptamer (5′- GGC GCC AAA CAG GAC CAC CAT GAC AAT TAC CCA TAC CAC CTC ATT ATG CCC CAT CTC CGC -3′) was used for the detection of MC-LR. Based on the specific binding interaction of the nucleic acid aptamer with MC-LR to burst the fluorescence of ZnTCPP@MOF films, achieved the determination of MC-LR in the pond water (Fig. 7B). The method had a wide linear range of 1.02 × 10−3 to 1.02 × 104 μg/L and a detection limit was 3.88 × 10−4 μg/L. A novel method with high sensitivity, high specificity and low detection limit was prepared using the interaction of nucleic acid aptamer with MC-LR.

MOFs are highly porous materials that can be used for effective adsorption and separation of analytes, making them ideal sensing materials for detecting MCs. MOFs can also be combined with other functional materials, endowing them with excellent conductivity, luminescence, and catalytic activity. Overall, the use of MOFs and their composite materials as sample pretreatment or chromatographic sensing materials in the assays of MCs is a promising research direction. However, there are still some shortcomings in the application of MOFs in fluorescence assays of MCs, for example, some MOFs may lack sufficient stability in biological samples and are susceptible to interference that affects the accuracy of assay results. And some MOFs may have lower assay sensitivity, which may limit their application in the assays of MCs. Future research can focus on improving the fluorescence quenching efficiency, quenching time, selectivity and specificity, as well as biocompatibility of MOFs to enhance their application performance in fluorescence assays of MCs.

In addition to traditional gold nanomaterials, QDs, carbon nanomaterials, and MOFs, there are other nanomaterials for MCs. By combining nanomaterials with biomolecules or chemical substances, the sensitivity and accuracy of assay can be further improved.

In recent years, magnetic nanoparticles have been widely used in the assays of MCs due to their special magnetic properties, which can achieve rapid separation and enrichment of samples in complex matrices, greatly improving the sensitivity of assays and reducing the interference of other substances (Di, et al., 2020). Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are particles that combine the properties of both MNPs and nanoparticles that respond to magnetic fields (Mohammed, Gomaa, Ragab, & Zhu, 2016; Nadar, Patil, & Rohra, 2020). The chemical properties of magnetic nanoparticles depend on their constituent materials. Common magnetic nanoparticles are usually composed of magnetic materials such as iron, nickel and cobalt (Yu et al., 2019). The following are some common chemical properties of magnetic nanoparticles: (1) The surface of magnetic nanoparticles is usually modified with different functional groups to enhance their dispersibility, stability or biocompatibility. Common surface functional groups include carboxyl, amino, and sulfur groups. (2) Magnetic nanoparticles at the nanoscale are more susceptible to oxidation in air to generate oxides due to their large specific surface area. (3) The magnetic stability of magnetic nanoparticles depends on their crystal structure, size and surface properties. The properties and stability of magnetic nanoparticles are affected under the action of an applied magnetic field. Surface modification of magnetic nanoparticles not only improves their stability, but also improves their dispersion and biocompatibility in aqueous solution. The chemical nature of magnetic nanomaterials influences the way in which they are surface modified and functionalized, and the release and detection of fluorescent signals can be achieved by modifying fluorescent probes on the surface of magnetic nanomaterials. This strategy of combining magnetic nanomaterials and fluorescent probes provides a new way for indirect fluorescence detection. In this case, a typical example was that Li et al. proposed an indirect fluorescence strategy for the assays of MC-RR (Li et al., 2023). The project used the MC-RR pseudo-fragment arginine as a template, which was loaded on the surface of ZnFe2O4-NP. Finally, the indirect fluorescent sensing mechanism (IFSM) technology was applied to paper-based microfluidic chip to develop a multifunctional analysis platform. The detection limit of the actual water samples was 0.43 μg/L in 20.0 min, and the fluorescence intensity was linearly related to the MC-RR concentration when the MC-RR concentration was 0.5–50.0 μg/L. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the MC-RR was linearly related to the MC-RR concentration. The IFSM system constructed in this project was highly versatile and could be widely applied to a variety of species that cannot interact directly with fluorescence substrates. The results of this research could provide a reference for highly sensitive in situ assays and lay the foundation for its application in practice.

Europium (Eu) and its compounds are widely used in the assays of MCs. The characteristics of Eu include long-lived excited and emitting states, narrow emission peaks and large molar absorptivity. Therefore, Eu show high sensitivity and selectivity in fluorescence assays. Based on the fluorescence properties of Eu, a new method with high sensitivity and selectivity for the assays of MCs can be developed. In this case, a typical example was, Liu et al. proposed a fluorescence immunochromatography technique based on Eu fluorescent microspheres for the assays of MC-LR in drinking water and freshwater foods (Liu, et al., 2019). MC-LR artificial antigen, MC-LR bovine serum albumin and goat antibody were fluorescently traced with Eu nanospheres, respectively (Fig. 8 A). On this basis, the fluorescence microspheres immunochromatographic assay method was further optimized to achieve rapid assay of MC-LR within 15.0 min. The linear range of the method was 0.1–5.0 μg/L, and the detection limit was 0.0542 μg/L. The implementation of this project could lay the foundation for the realization of MC-LR highly sensitive, fast and easy rapid assay. In addition, by modifying antibodies or ligands on the surface of fluorescent microspheres, highly specific recognition of target molecules can be achieved, avoiding interference by interfering substances. Fluorescent microsphere immunochromatographic assay has high fluorescence intensity and can detect low concentration of target molecules; it is easy and fast to operate and suitable for rapid screening and quantitative detection; at the same time, it can carry a variety of fluorescent markers to achieve multi-detection, which improves the diversity and accuracy of detection.

Different from the above-mentioned nanomaterials, fluorescence metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, easy surface modification and other advantages, which have broad application prospects in the assays of MCs (Wu, Sun, Huang, Lai, & Xiong, 2021). Among them, DNA template copper nanoclusters have attracted extensive attention because of their high specificity. Copper nanoclusters can simplify the preparation process without strictly controlling the temperature or accurately designing the DNA probe. A typical example was, Zhang et al. reported a novel copper nanoclusters based on double stranded DNA (dsDNA) for the assays of MC-LR in actual water samples (Zhang, et al., 2020). The maximum emission wavelength of dsDNA fluorescence probe was ∼575 nm. The aptamer (5′-GGC GCC AAA CAG GAC CAC CAT GAC AAT TAC CCA TAC CAC CTC ATT ATG CCC CAT CTC CGC-3′) was used for the detection of MC-LR. When the MC-LR was combined with aptamer, its fluorescence intensity at ∼575 nm was decreased significantly, indicating its conformational change in double stranded DNA (Fig. 8B). Using the change of fluorescence intensity, the concentration of MC-LR could be monitored from 10.0 to 1.0 × 106 μg/L and the detection limit was 4.8 × 10−3 μg/L. This method could detect MCs with high sensitivity and selectivity without complex DNA sequence design, fluorescence labeling and complex experimental methods.

Each of these fluorescent nanomaterials described above has its own characteristics and advantages, for example, gold nanomaterials have a surface-enhanced Raman scattering effect for improved detection sensitivity and tunable surface plasmon resonance properties for the detection of a wide range of biomolecules; quantum dots have a narrow emission spectrum that provides high sensitivity for detection, and excellent optical properties and stability for accurate and rapid detection of the presence of MCs; carbon nanomaterials have high fluorescence quantum yield and good biocompatibility; they can be used as biomarkers and biosensors, which can help to achieve highly sensitive detection of MCs; metal-organic skeletons have a large pore structure and high surface area, which is conducive to the adsorption and detection of the target molecules; they have a high degree of selectivity and sensitivity, and can be used to detect MCs at low concentrations; magnetic nanomaterials are magnetic and can be used for facile separation and enrichment of target molecules, combined with fluorescent labelling they enable rapid and efficient detection of MCs and are suitable for analysis of complex samples. The combined use of their features in practical applications can achieve high sensitivity, accuracy, biocompatibility, selectivity and rapid separation, providing a diverse and efficient solution for the detection of MCs. However, some nanomaterials may lose stability under specific environmental conditions, affecting the accuracy of assay, and further research is needed on the biocompatibility of organisms, which may pose certain toxicity and biosafety risks. Despite some challenges and limitations, these innovative methods still provide new avenues for sensitive in situ assay and practical applications.

MCs is common toxin in lakes and coastal marine environments, and is affected by blue-green algae blooms (Krishnan & Mou, 2021). The existence of MCs and their harm to human health is currently a hot issue in the field of global ecological and environmental health (Lad, et al., 2022; Massey et al., 2018; Preece, Hardy, Moore, & Bryan, 2016). As a very promising and emerging method, nanomaterials-based fluorescence technology has been widely studied and applied to the determination of MCs in seafood and aquaculture-related matrices, which not only contributes to safeguarding food safety and aquaculture quality, but also helps to provide early warning and prevention of the potential hazards of MCs to public health and aquaculture. In this context, this article systematically analyses recent advances in the application of nanomaterials in the fluorescence detection of MCs in seafood and aquaculture-associated matrices in recent years, and summarizes the mechanisms of nanomaterial-based fluorescence assays in MCs. Although fluorescence assays based on nanomaterials have advantages over traditional methods, its application still needs to pay attention to the following issues: (1) Currently the development of fluorescence assays for MCs is often at the sample spiking and recovery stage in the laboratory, involving complex pre-treatment process of filtration, extraction and purification, as well as these assays are only applicable in aqueous matrices. However, it is also an indisputable fact that MCs are present in a wide variety of organisms and agriculture-related matrices, thus the development of more powerful, stable, and sensitive methods for the determination of MCs is needed (Liu, Li, et al., 2022). (2) Simple nanomaterials cannot be used directly for the determination of MCs, and require metal doping, surface modification, and the establishment of suitable donor-acceptor pairs, which raises the cost and operational difficulty of practical applications. And the stability of fluorescent nanomaterials in complex environments is also a factor that should be considered (Khan, et al., 2023). (3) Nanomaterials are susceptible to non-target binding interference when directly exposed to the environment due to their large specific surface area, although the binding of aptamers shows higher specificity for MCs, which also limits their applicability and practical performance in the environment (Khan, et al., 2021). (4) Nanomaterial-based fluorescence methods, most of which are based on the traditional Stokes fluorescence mode (short-wave excitation, long-wave emission), are susceptible to suffer from the interference of the actual sample background signals. Therefore, anti-Stokes emission (long-wave excitation, short-wave emission) based on upconverted nanomaterials may be an effective way to avoid the interference of autofluorescence and light scattering in seafood and aquaculture-related matrices (Zhou, Lu, Yang, & Wu, 2019). (5) Nanomaterials may have some potential environmental impacts and risks, especially their enrichment in water bodies may be harmful to aquatic organisms, which in turn may affect food safety and public health through the biological chain. It is therefore necessary to fully assess the ecotoxicity, biodegradability and bioconcentration of nanomaterials, strengthen environmental monitoring and risk assessment, as well as propose corresponding countermeasures to ensure their safety and sustainability (Keller, Ehrens, Zheng, & Nowack, 2023).

We believe that this systematic review will help researchers understand the latest progress in fluorescence assays of MCs based on nanomaterials, and develop new, sensitive, fast, and cost-effective methods. At present, fluorescence assay technology based on nanomaterials has made some progress and has shown high sensitivity and accuracy under laboratory conditions (Li, Gao, et al., 2023; Nie et al., 2023). With the continuous development of nanomaterial-based fluorescence analysis technology, we may see more multifunctional nanomaterials designed for specific application needs in the future. In particular, the combination of functional units such as fluorescent probes, targeting molecules, signal amplification, and even machine learning techniques for big data and multimodal detection technologies, integrated into portable detection devices, is expected to enable on-site real-time monitoring of MCs in outdoor environments (Shen, Wei, et al., 2022; Shen, Wei, et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023). However, in order to achieve a new type of portable detection device based on nanomaterials, some challenges still need to be overcome, such as the stability, cost-effectiveness, and feasibility of practical applications of the device. It is believed that in the near future, this type of equipment is expected to be further developed and improved to enable on-site detection of the presence of MCs, which is of great significance in ensuring the safety of seafood and aquaculture.

Fengjiao He: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft. Chao Nie: Formal analysis, Writing-original draft. Sha Liu: Formal analysis. Guojian Wu: Formal analysis. Yizhong Shen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition. Nannan Qiu: Formal analysis, Writing-review & editing.
